---
author: Emad Saberi
title: Demonstrating DSGE modelling with MacroModelling.jl
date: 2026/05/11
format:
 html:
  embed-resources: true
  theme: simplex
---

_For presentation to Dr. Giacomo Candian_

Paper: [Loss Function-Based Evaluation of DSGE Models (Frank Schorfheide, 2000)](http://proxy2.hec.ca/login?url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2678564)

# Paper Summary

Given DSGE models are almost certainly misspecified, how to coherently and formally evaluate and compare them?

Schorfheide proposes evaluating DSGE models through Loss Functions, compares Cash in Advance and Portfolio Adjustment Cost. VAR serves as flexible reference model

1. Compute posterior distributions via MH
2. Derive posteriors over population characteristics as a weighted mixture across the models
3. Evaluate three loss functions and rank model predictions
4. Find loss-function based parameter estimates that achieve best fit

# Reproduction, Framework

Only focused on reproducing **CIA Model** with the **MacroModelling.jl** package, goal is to estimate posterior distributions of parameters and compare with paper.


```{julia}
#| label: "julia and package version info"
#===================SETUP ENVIRONMENT=====================#
pwd()
run(`julia --version`)
using Pkg; Pkg.activate(joinpath("..","..")); Pkg.status()
```

## MacroModelling.jl: background

[Github link](https://github.com/thorek1/MacroModelling.jl)
[Workshop link](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/workshop.html)
[Official Documentation](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/MacroModelling.jl/stable/)

### Description

`MacroModelling.jl` is a Julia package for developing and solving dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) models. v0.1 released 2023.

- `MacroModelling.jl` serves as a tool for handling the complexities involved with DSGE modelling (forward-looking expectations, nonlinearity, and high dimensionality).
- The goal is to reduce coding time and speed up model development by providing functions for working with discrete-time DSGE models.
- The package allows the user to work with nonlinear model solutions ... and estimate the model using gradient based samplers (e.g. NUTS, or HMC).
- Target audience includes central bankers, regulators, graduate students, and others working in academia with an interest in DSGE modelling.

### The Author

[Thore Kockerols](https://www.thorekockerols.eu/)

Senior Economist at European Central Bank, PhD Econ (2018 Universite Paris Sarbonne)

[Package Presentation at JuliaCon24](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4NkJBDVxMWo)

Area of research and latest publications:
[Leaning Against the Wind: Macroprudential Policy and the Financial Cycle](https://doi.org/10.2866/744981)

## Replication

```{julia}
#========================DATA TRANSFORMATIONS=============================#
# %% packages
using AxisKeys

import CSV: read
import DataFrames: DataFrame, describe

# %% load data
dat = read("assets/data/FS2000_data.csv", DataFrame);
dat |> describe |> pretty_table
```

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% Transform to KeyedArray
data = KeyedArray(Array(dat)',
    Variable=Symbol.("log_" .* names(dat)),
    Time=1:size(dat, 1)
);

# %% logs
data = log.(data);

# %% declare observables
observables = sort(Symbol.("log_" .* names(dat)));
data = data(observables, :)
```

```{julia}
#========================INITIALIZE MACROMODEL=============================#
import MacroModelling

# %% define model
MacroModelling.@model FS2000 begin
    dA[0] = exp(γ + z_e_a * e_a[x])

    log(m[0]) = (1 - ρ) * log(mst) + ρ * log(m[-1]) + z_e_m * e_m[x]

    -P[0] / (c[1] * P[1] * m[0]) + β * P[1] * (α * exp(-α * (γ + log(e[1]))) * k[0]^(α - 1) * n[1]^(1 - α) + (1 - δ) * exp(-(γ + log(e[1])))) / (c[2] * P[2] * m[1]) = 0

    W[0] = l[0] / n[0]

    -(ψ / (1 - ψ)) * (c[0] * P[0] / (1 - n[0])) + l[0] / n[0] = 0

    R[0] = P[0] * (1 - α) * exp(-α * (γ + z_e_a * e_a[x])) * k[-1]^α * n[0]^(-α) / W[0]

    1 / (c[0] * P[0]) - β * P[0] * (1 - α) * exp(-α * (γ + z_e_a * e_a[x])) * k[-1]^α * n[0]^(1 - α) / (m[0] * l[0] * c[1] * P[1]) = 0

    c[0] + k[0] = exp(-α * (γ + z_e_a * e_a[x])) * k[-1]^α * n[0]^(1 - α) + (1 - δ) * exp(-(γ + z_e_a * e_a[x])) * k[-1]

    P[0] * c[0] = m[0]

    m[0] - 1 + d[0] = l[0]

    e[0] = exp(z_e_a * e_a[x])

    y[0] = k[-1]^α * n[0]^(1 - α) * exp(-α * (γ + z_e_a * e_a[x]))

    gy_obs[0] = dA[0] * y[0] / y[-1]

    gp_obs[0] = (P[0] / P[-1]) * m[-1] / dA[0]

    log_gy_obs[0] = log(gy_obs[0])

    log_gp_obs[0] = log(gp_obs[0])

end
```

```{julia}
# %% define parameters
MacroModelling.@parameters FS2000 begin
    α = 0.356
    β = 0.993
    γ = 0.0085
    mst = 1.0002
    ρ = 0.129
    ψ = 0.65
    δ = 0.01
    z_e_a = 0.035449
    z_e_m = 0.008862
end

```

```{julia}
#============================SET UP SAMPLER=============================#
# %% Sampling imports
import Turing
import Turing: NUTS

import MacroModelling: Beta, Normal, InverseGamma

```

Note: Turing.jl is the julia workhorse for probabilistic programming and sampling. MacroModelling.jl is designed to work well with Turing. A solved MacroModel provides a defined log likelihood function which feeds into Turing nicely.

Workflow becomes:
1. Define model, parameters, solve for steady state, linearize with MacroModelling.jl. MacroModelling.jl provides a kalman-filter log likelihood function with `get_loglikelihood(mode, data, parameters)`
2. Feed the custom log likelihood function to Turing.jl. Assuming the solved model's likelihood is first order differentiable, specify the sampler as NUTS
3. Select ~1000 samples with NUTS to describe posterior space with.

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% Specify prior distributions
prior_dists = [
    Beta(0.356, 0.02, μσ=true),           # α
    Beta(0.993, 0.002, μσ=true),          # β
    Normal(0.0085, 0.003),                  # γ
    Normal(1.0002, 0.007),                  # mst
    Beta(0.129, 0.223, μσ=true),          # ρ
    Beta(0.65, 0.05, μσ=true),            # ψ
    Beta(0.01, 0.005, μσ=true),           # δ
    InverseGamma(0.035449, Inf, μσ=true), # z_e_a
    InverseGamma(0.008862, Inf, μσ=true)  # z_e_m
];
```

^ From Schorfheide 2000

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% define sampling model
Turing.@model function FS2000_loglik_func(prior_dists, data, m; verbose=false)

    parameters ~ Turing.arraydist(prior_dists)

    Turing.@addlogprob! MacroModelling.get_loglikelihood(m, data, parameters)
end

# %% Specify sampler parameters
FS2000_loglik = FS2000_loglik_func(prior_dists, data, FS2000);
n_samples = 1000
sample_method = NUTS() #DSGE models usually differentiable
```

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% sample
chain_NUTS = cache_or_compute("assets/cache/chain_NUTS.jls") do
    Turing.sample(FS2000_loglik,
        sample_method,
        n_samples,
        initial_params=FS2000.parameter_values)
end
```

```{julia}
#| output: false
#============================MAKING SENSE OF POSTERIOR=============================#
# %% Inspection Imports
import StatsPlots: plot, savefig
import MacroModelling: get_parameters
import Turing: replacenames

# %% replacing posterior names
paramlist = get_parameters(FS2000)
chain_NUTS_rn = replacenames(chain_NUTS, Dict(["parameters[$i]" for i in 1:length(paramlist)] .=> get_parameters(FS2000)))
```

```{julia}
#| output: false
# %% plot
chain_NUTS_plot = plot(chain_NUTS_rn)

# %%
savefig(chain_NUTS_plot, "assets/plots/chain_NUTS_plot.png")
```

![[../assets/plots/chain_NUTS_plot.png]]
